en.extract:
The orange-tufted spiderhunter (Arachnothera flammifera) is a species of bird in the family Nectariniidae. It is endemic to the Philippines found only on Mindanao and Eastern Visayas. It was originally considered a subspecies of the little spiderhunter. 
This species was Peter Kaestner's 10,000th species of bird seen.


Description and taxonomy

This species was formerly conspecific with the little spiderhunter and pale spiderhunter. This species is differentiated by its pale gray throat and belly, its eponymous flame orange tufts and its higher pitched song.


Subspecies
Two subspecies are recognized:

A. f. flammifera – Found on  Mindanao (and its surrounding small islands)  Bohol, Leyte. Bohol. Biliran and Samar
A. f. randi –Found on Basilan; has a longer bill


Ecology and behavior
This species' diet has not yet been comprehensively studied but it is assumed to have a similar diet as the formerly conspecific little spiderhunter which feeds on nectar, insects and spiders.Has been known to show nectar robbing. Typically forages alone or in pairs in the lower levels of vegetation. 
Not much is known about its breeding habits but birds collected in breeding condition with enlarged gonads collected in April and May.


Habitat and conservation status
It is found in tropical moist lowland forest up to 1,000 meters (3,300 ft) above sea level. 
IUCN has assessed this bird as least-concern species. This species is generally uncommon but apparently shows a tolerance for degraded habitat and plantations. 
Occurs in a few protected areas like Pasonanca Natural Park, Mount Apo and Mount Kitanglad on Mindanao,  Rajah Sikatuna Protected Landscape in Bohol and Samar Island Natural Park but actual protection and enforcement from illegal logging and hunting are lax.


References
Moyle, R.G., S.S. Taylor, C.H. Oliveros, H.C. Lim, C.L. Haines, M.A. Rahman, and F.H. Sheldon. 2011. Diversification of an endemic Southeast Asian genus: phylogenetic relationships of the spiderhunters (Nectariniidae: Arachnothera). Auk 128: 777–788.

The '''orange-tufted spiderhunter''' ('''''Arachnothera flammifera''''') is a species of bird in the family [[Nectariniidae]]. It is endemic to the Philippines found only on [[Mindanao]] and [[Eastern Visayas]]. It was originally considered a subspecies of the [[little spiderhunter]]. 

This species was [[Peter Kaestner]]'s 10,000th species of bird seen.<ref>{{Cite news |title=With an Orange-Tufted Spiderhunter, Birder Breaks Record for Sighting |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/03/11/science/birds-birdwatching-record-kaestner.html |work=[[The New York Times]]}}</ref>

== Description and taxonomy ==
[[File:Orange-tufted Spiderhunter Tufts.jpg|left|thumb|An orange-tufted spiderhunter displaying its tufts]]
This species was formerly conspecific with the [[little spiderhunter]] and [[pale spiderhunter]]. This species is differentiated by its pale gray throat and belly, its [[Eponym|eponymous]] flame orange tufts and its higher pitched song.

=== Subspecies ===
Two [[subspecies]] are recognized:

* ''A. f. flammifera'' – Found on  [[Mindanao]] (and its surrounding small islands)  [[Bohol]], [[Leyte]]. [[Bohol]]. [[Biliran]] and [[Samar]]
* ''A. f.'' ''randi'' –Found on [[Basilan]]; has a longer bill

== Ecology and behavior ==
This species' diet has not yet been comprehensively studied but it is assumed to have a similar diet as the formerly conspecific [[little spiderhunter]] which feeds on nectar, insects and spiders.Has been known to show [[nectar robbing]]. Typically forages alone or in pairs in the lower levels of vegetation. 

Not much is known about its breeding habits but birds collected in breeding condition with enlarged gonads collected in April and May.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=del Hoyo |first1=Josep |last2=Collar |first2=Nigel |last3=Kirwan |first3=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=Orange-tufted Spiderhunter (Arachnothera flammifera), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/ortspi1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.ortspi1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cheke |first1=Robert |last2=Mann |first2=Clive |last3=Kirwan |first3=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=Little Spiderhunter (Arachnothera longirostra), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/litspi1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.litspi1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
It is found in tropical moist lowland forest up to {{Convert|1000|m|sp=us}} above sea level. 

IUCN has assessed this bird as [[least-concern species]]. This species is generally uncommon but apparently shows a tolerance for degraded habitat and plantations. 

Occurs in a few protected areas like [[Pasonanca Natural Park]], [[Mount Apo]] and [[Mount Kitanglad]] on Mindanao,  [[Rajah Sikatuna Protected Landscape]] in Bohol and [[Samar Island Natural Park]] but actual protection and enforcement from [[illegal logging]] and hunting are lax.<ref>{{Cite report |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/103778923/132044412 |title=Arachnothera flammifera: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2018: e.T103778923A132044412 |last=IUCN |date=2018-08-09 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2018-2.rlts.t103778923a132044412.en |language=en}}</ref>

==References==
*Moyle, R.G., S.S. Taylor, C.H. Oliveros, H.C. Lim, C.L. Haines, M.A. Rahman, and F.H. Sheldon. 2011. Diversification of an endemic Southeast Asian genus: phylogenetic relationships of the spiderhunters (Nectariniidae: Arachnothera). Auk 128: 777–788.
